md:
One might naively expect "recapitulate" to have semantically diverged from "capitulate" and then attempt to draw a tenuous semantic connection to the latter. Perhaps the word became an overdramatic way to describe a "capitulation" to previously ingested information?

That would have been a funny explanation, but it's actually "recapitulate" that remained anchored to its original meaning, and "capitulate" that drifted. Both words derive from the Latin verb *capitulare*, which describes the act of organizing written content into chapters under headings (*capitulum*: little head), and "re" + "capitulare" is a fairly accurate etymological description of "recapitulate". "capitulate", on the other hand, was given the meaning that it has today because the act of "capitulare"-ing was significant when drawing up treaties of surrender, and the word eventually grew to encompass the acts that took place before the actual "capitulare"-ing was done.
